title: Guiding Language Models of Code with Global Context using Monitors (Monitor-Guided Decoding)
claim: Static-analysis monitor (language-server) derives type-consistent continuation sets during decoding; SantaCoder-1.1B + MGD beats text-davinci-003 on compilation rate and next-identifier match on PragmaticCode Java; improvements consistent across model scales. The flagship executor-in-the-decode-loop result at <2B scale.

title: CRANE: Reasoning with constrained LLM generation
claim: Theory: restricting output to an answer-only grammar diminishes reasoning; augmenting the grammar with carefully designed additional rules preserves reasoning while keeping constraints. Alternating unconstrained-reasoning/constrained-output gains up to 10pp accuracy on GSM-symbolic and FOLIO over both constrained and unconstrained baselines on the SAME model — an accuracy result, NOT resource-matched: Table 1 shows CRANE emits MORE tokens than unconstrained CoT in nearly every arm (e.g. Qwen2.5-1.5B 131.3 vs 128.97; R1-Distill-Qwen-7B 235.78 vs 212.24) plus constrained-decoder work.
locator: Table 1 (GSM-Symbolic accuracy + avg tokens), FOLIO results section; re-checked at arxiv.org/html/2502.09061 2026-07-11

title: Grammar-Aligned Decoding
claim: Greedy grammar-masking (GCD) distorts the LM's distribution — grammatical but low-likelihood outputs; ASAp adaptive sampling provably converges to the LM's grammar-conditioned distribution and produces higher-likelihood constrained outputs.

title: Synchromesh: Reliable code generation from pre-trained language models
venue: ICLR (venue per prior verified review; arXiv page fetched today)
claim: Constrained Semantic Decoding via 'completion engines': partial-output constraints covering syntax, scope, typing and contextual logic (beyond grammar) with no retraining; substantial gains in accuracy and run-time-error prevention on SQL/Vega-Lite/SMCalFlow with GPT-3/Codex. The formal template for an engine-derived-continuation-set decoder.

title: Robust Text-to-SQL Generation with Execution-Guided Decoding
claim: Executing partially generated SQL during decoding prunes faulty candidates without retraining; applied to 4 model families; then-SOTA 83.8% execution accuracy on WikiSQL. Earliest clean executor-in-the-decode-loop precedent.

title: Type-Constrained Code Generation with Language Models
claim: Prefix automata + type-inference search over inhabitable types enforce well-typedness during decoding (TypeScript); compilation errors reduced by more than half and functional correctness increased for synthesis/translation/repair across LLM sizes and families. Executor = type system, sound enforcement.

title: IterGen: Iterative Semantic-aware Structured LLM Generation with Backtracking
claim: Grammar-symbol-addressed backtracking with KV-cache state maintenance lets a decoder resample fragments violating semantic predicates; +18.5% mean over SOTA grammar-guided generation on SQL, +17.8% on Vega-Lite.

title: SynCode: LLM Generation with Grammar Augmentation
claim: Offline DFA mask store over CFG terminals; eliminates JSON syntax errors, reduces Python/Go syntax errors 96.07%. Grammar-only transport layer (no executor).

title: XGrammar: Flexible and Efficient Structured Generation Engine for Large Language Models
claim: Context-independent-token precheck + persistent stack + GPU overlap gives up to 100x speedup and near-zero-overhead structured generation — the GRAMMAR-MASK transport cost of CD is engineering-solved. Scope: measured for context-free-grammar masking only; establishes nothing about semantic-engine calls, per-token executor/kernel work, or backtracking cost (those are open for executor-coupled CD).

title: Let Me Speak Freely? A Study on the Impact of Format Restrictions on Performance of Large Language Models
venue: arXiv (EMNLP 2024 industry track per prior review)
claim: Significant decline in reasoning ability under format restriction; stricter constraints correlate with greater loss on reasoning tasks. The measured cost side of naive constrained output.

title: From Hallucination to Structure Snowballing: The Alignment Tax of Constrained Decoding in LLM Reflection
claim: Outlines-based constrained decoding on Qwen3-8B achieves near-perfect syntactic alignment while degrading semantic error detection in reflection workflows ('structure snowballing') — constraint satisfaction can interfere with self-correction without additional training.

title: PICARD: Parsing Incrementally for Constrained Auto-Regressive Decoding from Language Models
claim: Incremental parsing with lexing/parsing/type-checking modes (schema-aware, i.e., partially semantic) rejects inadmissible tokens; T5-3B to then-SOTA on Spider/CoSQL; Spider-dev execution errors 12%->2%.

title: KBLaM: Knowledge Base augmented Language Model
venue: ICLR (venue per prior verified review 2026-07-08; arXiv page fetched today)
claim: KB triples -> continuous KV 'knowledge tokens' via pre-trained sentence encoders + trained linear adapters, read through rectangular attention; >10K triples into an 8B host, one A100, linear scaling in KB size; instruction-tunes the projection, base weights untouched; claims attention weights give 'interpretable insights' into knowledge use.

title: Introducing KBLaM: Bringing plug-and-play external knowledge to LLMs (Microsoft Research blog)
claim: States 'attention weights provide clear insights into how the model utilizes knowledge tokens... mimicking a soft retrieval process'; hallucination-reduction claimed only for KBs > ~200 triples; 'work to be done before it can be deployed at scale'; trained primarily on factual QA pairs.

title: AtlasKV: Augmenting LLMs with Billion-Scale Knowledge Graphs in 20GB VRAM
claim: KG2KV + HiKVP integrate ~1B KG triples into LLM attention with sub-linear time/memory, <20GB VRAM, no external retriever, no retraining for new knowledge. The KBLaM-line scaling successor.

title: Memory Layers at Scale
claim: Trainable sparsely-activated KV memory layers complementing dense FFN: models beat dense baselines with >2x the compute budget, and beat MoE at matched compute AND params; up to 128B memory params, 1T tokens, base models to 8B; gains concentrated on factual tasks. Honest accounting: params up, FLOPs matched — a trained-from-pretraining architecture, not a bolt-on store.

title: KV Cache Steering for Controlling Frozen LLMs (v1: KV Cache Steering for Inducing Reasoning in Small Language Models)
claim: Training-free ONE-SHOT intervention on the KV cache (vectors distilled from GPT-4o reasoning traces) induces explicit multi-step reasoning in small models (SmolLM2-360M-class per paper experiments) and transfers reasoning styles; more stable to hyperparameters and cheaper than continuous activation steering. Behaviour-level, not content-level, KV write at our band.

title: Larimar: Large Language Models with Episodic Memory Control
claim: External episodic memory conditioning a decoder gives one-shot knowledge updates without retraining; editing accuracy comparable to competitive baselines with 8-10x speedups; selective forgetting and context-length generalization.

title: Generalization through Memorization: Nearest Neighbor Language Models
claim: Datastore keyed by the model's OWN hidden states; WikiText-103 ppl 18.65->15.79 with no training. Interface-locality anchor: external memory works when addressed in the host's native representation.

title: Memorizing Transformers
claim: kNN attention into non-differentiable memory of the model's own past KV pairs; matches models ~5x larger (claimed at that ratio). Same interface-locality law.

title: MeKi: Memory-based Expert Knowledge Injection for Efficient LLM Scaling
claim: Token-level memory experts per transformer layer re-parameterized into static ROM lookup tables; outperforms dense baselines at identical inference speed for edge deployment. Trained memory, storage-for-compute trade at small scale.

title: Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT (ROME)
claim: Causal tracing localizes factual recall to mid-layer MLPs at subject tokens; rank-one MLP edit changes single associations on GPT-2 XL / GPT-J with specificity+generalization other methods sacrifice.

title: Mass-Editing Memory in a Transformer (MEMIT)
venue: ICLR (arXiv page fetched; venue per ICLR proceedings)
claim: Scales direct weight editing to thousands of associations on GPT-J-6B and GPT-NeoX-20B, orders of magnitude beyond prior work.

title: Does Localization Inform Editing? Surprising Differences in Causality-Based Localization vs. Knowledge Editing in Language Models
claim: Causal-tracing localization does NOT predict which layer is best to edit; edit-layer choice unrelated to tracing signal. Direct dissociation between a causal-localization instrument and an intervention outcome — load-bearing for provenance practice.

title: Evaluating the Ripple Effects of Knowledge Editing in Language Models
claim: RippleEdits (5K edits): current parametric editing methods fail to propagate logically entailed changes; a simple in-context editing baseline obtains the best scores.

title: Model Editing at Scale leads to Gradual and Catastrophic Forgetting
claim: Sequential ROME/MEMIT editing: progressive forgetting of earlier edits and downstream ability, then abrupt collapse — editing does not scale as a maintenance channel.

title: AlphaEdit: Null-Space Constrained Knowledge Editing for Language Models
venue: ICLR (Oral; Outstanding Paper per conference materials)
claim: Projecting edit perturbations onto the null space of preserved-knowledge keys boosts locate-then-edit methods by avg 36.7% (LLaMA3, GPT2-XL, GPT-J), especially sequential editing — current SOTA of the line; still fact-level rewrites, not rule injection.

title: A Comprehensive Study of Knowledge Editing for Large Language Models
claim: Taxonomy: external-knowledge / merge-into-model / edit-intrinsic; KnowEdit benchmark; in-depth knowledge-location analysis. The field's consolidation reference for editing practice.

title: Inference-Time Intervention: Eliciting Truthful Answers from a Language Model
claim: Shifting activations along probe directions in a few attention heads: Alpaca TruthfulQA 32.5% -> 65.1%. The strongest single steering positive; behaviour-level.

title: Steering Language Models With Activation Engineering (ActAdd)
claim: Contrast-pair activation additions steer topic/sentiment without optimization; the method-family origin for CAA-style steering.

title: Steering Llama 2 via Contrastive Activation Addition
claim: Mean difference of residual activations over contrast pairs, applied at all post-prompt positions, alters behaviours on Llama-2-Chat over and on top of finetuning/system prompts with minimal capability loss (as self-reported).

title: Representation Engineering: A Top-Down Approach to AI Transparency
claim: Reading vectors / steering at representation level across safety-relevant behaviours; the RepE umbrella the reliability critiques test.

title: Function Vectors in Large Language Models
claim: Causal mediation over attention heads finds compact vectors that trigger execution of an ICL-demonstrated task zero-shot in novel contexts — tasks live as transportable activation-space objects.

title: Analyzing the Generalization and Reliability of Steering Vectors
venue: arXiv (NeurIPS 2024 per listing; arXiv page fetched)
claim: Steerability is highly variable across inputs; spurious biases substantially drive per-input effectiveness; brittleness to reasonable prompt changes; 'substantial limitations both in- and out-of-distribution'.

title: Towards Reliable Evaluation of Behavior Steering Interventions in LLMs
claim: Four evaluation pitfalls (context relevance, likelihood accounting, standardization, baselines); under a corrected pipeline some RepE interventions are less effective than previously reported.

title: AxBench: Steering LLMs? Even Simple Baselines Outperform Sparse Autoencoders
claim: On Gemma-2-2B/9B concept steering: PROMPTING outperforms all representation methods, finetuning second; SAEs not competitive; difference-in-means best for detection. The matched-baseline discipline result for the whole steering family.

title: Steering off Course: Reliability Challenges in Steering Language Models
claim: DoLa / function vectors / task vectors on 36 models (14 families, 1.5B-70B): substantial variability; many models show no improvement or degradation; 'fundamental flaws in the assumptions underlying these methods'.

title: Understanding (Un)Reliability of Steering Vectors in Language Models
venue: ICLR Workshop (Foundation Models in the Wild)
claim: Steering fails when the target behaviour is not represented by a coherent linear direction: cosine similarity of training activation differences and pos/neg separation predict steerability. Mechanistic cause of unreliability = representation geometry, not extraction quality.

title: Improving Rule-based Reasoning in LLMs using Neurosymbolic Representations
claim: Encode hidden states into VSA vectors, run symbolic algorithms in vector space, decode and merge back: 88.6% lower cross-entropy and 15.4x more math problems solved vs CoT and supervised-fine-tuning/LoRA baselines while retaining other-benchmark performance. Single-group, narrow task suite; the direct H-RULE-ACT prior (and the nsk1-adjacent design).
locator: ACL Anthology abstract (immutable proceedings record; figures 88.6%/15.4x confirmed there 2026-07-11). Pinned to the Anthology because the arXiv 2502.01657 page is mutable and its current abstract reports different numbers.

title: ATLAS: Verifier-Guided Adaptive Latent Activation Steering for Efficient LLM Reasoning
claim: A trained lightweight verifier over latent states predicts step quality and adaptively triggers steering; beats vanilla decoding and fixed steering on math/coding while cutting test-time tokens. Executor-adjacent steering: the gate is learned, not deterministic.

title: K-Adapter: Infusing Knowledge into Pre-Trained Models with Adapters
claim: Frozen RoBERTa + parallel knowledge adapters (factual, linguistic); gains on relation classification, entity typing, QA — task-local wins, the KEPLM-era adapter baseline.

title: Plug-and-Play Knowledge Injection for Pre-trained Language Models
claim: Map-tuning trains a mapping of knowledge embeddings into a FROZEN downstream model's inputs; conventional injection fails in this paradigm, map-tuning works, domain-swappable mapping networks. Trained-bridge requirement again.

title: Editing Models with Task Arithmetic
claim: Task vectors (finetuned minus pretrained weights) compose by addition/negation to steer multi-task behaviour without retraining — weight-space procedures are somewhat modular.

title: LoraHub: Efficient Cross-Task Generalization via Dynamic LoRA Composition
claim: Gradient-free dynamic composition of task-trained LoRA modules for unseen BBH tasks; comparable to few-shot ICL with far fewer inference tokens. Procedure-adapters compose, with few-shot-level (not verified-soundness) reliability.

title: Fine-Tuning or Retrieval? Comparing Knowledge Injection in LLMs
claim: RAG consistently beats unsupervised fine-tuning for existing AND new knowledge; models struggle to learn new facts by fine-tuning without many paraphrase repetitions. Accounting: same-host accuracy comparison; does NOT establish matched lifecycle/tuning compute across arms, and tests KNOWLEDGE injection, not rules.

title: Does Fine-Tuning LLMs on New Knowledge Encourage Hallucinations?
claim: New-knowledge examples are learned much slower than known-knowledge ones, and as they ARE learned they linearly increase hallucination — fine-tuning teaches usage, pre-training stores knowledge.

title: Understanding Parametric Knowledge Injection in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (v2 title; v1 was titled 'The Role of Parametric Injection-A Systematic Study of Parametric Retrieval-Augmented Generation')
claim: LoRA-encoded documents (P-RAG) capture document-level semantics in deep FFN computation, give high-level guidance but LIMITED evidence consolidation; parametric alone does not consistently outperform token-level RAG — hybrid PT-RAG wins.
locator: Version-pinned to v2 (paper retitled between v1 and v2; both titles recorded above; v1/v2 titles confirmed at arXiv 2026-07-11).

title: Physics of Language Models: Part 3.2, Knowledge Manipulation
claim: Even when knowledge is perfectly stored, transformers fail simple classification/comparison over it without CoT at train+inference, and inverse search is ~0% — storage does not confer in-weights manipulation.

title: Towards Revealing the Mystery behind Chain of Thought: A Theoretical Perspective
claim: Bounded-depth transformers cannot directly answer basic arithmetic/equation tasks unless size grows super-polynomially in input length; constant-size autoregressive transformers suffice WITH CoT — multi-step inference must surface into token space (or an external engine).

title: Grokked Transformers are Implicit Reasoners: A Mechanistic Journey to the Edge of Generalization
claim: Parametric two-hop composition groks in-distribution but systematically FAILS OOD; comparison generalizes — circuit topology decides whether baked-in rule application transfers; grokked models can beat GPT-4-Turbo on in-domain search-heavy tasks.

title: The Reversal Curse: LLMs trained on 'A is B' fail to learn 'B is A'
claim: Directional storage: GPT-4 79% forward vs 33% reverse on celebrity relations; robust across sizes/families and to augmentation. Baked-in facts are not closed under the trivial inverse rule.

title: Lessons from Studying Two-Hop Latent Reasoning
claim: Llama-3-8B/GPT-4o fine-tuned on synthetic facts: composing two synthetic facts latently (no CoT) FAILS; synthetic+natural succeeds — latent capacity exists but is unreliable; guards against both spurious success (memorization) and spurious failure.

title: Hopping Too Late: Exploring the Limitations of Large Language Models on Multi-Hop Queries
claim: Bridge entity resolved early, second hop late; back-patching a later hidden state into an earlier layer rescues up to 66% of failed two-hop cases — layer ordering is a hard resource constraint on in-weights composition.

title: Attention is not Explanation
claim: Attention weights frequently uncorrelated with gradient feature-importance; adversarially different attention distributions yield identical predictions — attention maps are not faithful provenance.

title: Attention is not not Explanation
claim: Qualifies Jain & Wallace: existence of adversarial attention does not disprove usefulness; supplies four diagnostic tests (uniform baseline, seed variance, frozen-weight diagnostics, adversarial training). Net: attention claims need diagnostics, never stand alone.

title: Towards Best Practices of Activation Patching in Language Models: Metrics and Methods
claim: Varying patching metric (prob vs logit-diff vs KL) and corruption method leads to DISPARATE interpretability conclusions; prescribes best practices — causal-patching results are protocol-sensitive.

title: The Hydra Effect: Emergent Self-repair in Language Model Computations
claim: Ablating one attention layer causes downstream layers to compensate (self-repair even without dropout); late MLPs downregulate the top token — naive ablation-based attribution understates/mislocates causal pathways.

title: Is This the Subspace You Are Looking for? An Interpretability Illusion for Subspace Activation Patching
claim: Subspace patching can change behaviour by activating DORMANT parallel pathways causally disconnected from the model's normal computation — behavioural effect of an intervention does not certify the claimed mechanism.

title: Causal Abstraction: A Theoretical Foundation for Mechanistic Interpretability
claim: Unifies activation/path patching, causal mediation, causal scrubbing, causal tracing, circuit analysis, erasure, SAEs, steering under interchange-intervention-based causal abstraction — the theory the provenance instrumentation should be stated in.

title: Causal Scrubbing: a method for rigorously testing interpretability hypotheses
claim: Behaviour-preserving resampling ablations test whether a hypothesized causal graph explains model behaviour. Fetch today returned truncated page content (title/nav only) — method anchored via the causal-abstraction survey's verified unification list. UNVERIFIED-THIS-PASS at source.

title: Concept Bottleneck Models
claim: Predict interpretable concepts then predict from concepts; competitive accuracy with end-to-end models on x-ray grading/bird ID; supports test-time concept intervention.

title: Do Concept Bottleneck Models Learn as Intended?
claim: CBM concepts 'do not correspond to anything semantically meaningful in input space' — leakage: named bottleneck units fail interpretability/predictability/intervenability goals. Naming units is not provenance.

title: Concept Bottleneck Large Language Models
claim: CB layers built into LLMs: claims classification parity with black-box baselines plus concept detection, controlled generation, unlearning — the direct H-RULE-HL-adjacent architecture; self-reported, causal validation of the named units not established at scale.

title: [dedup pointer] retrieval/external-memory lineage (REALM, RETRO, InstructRetro, Atlas)
claim: Owned by the injection report + RAG review; RULE.md cites their interface-locality and absorption laws without re-verifying.

title: Logic-LM: Empowering Large Language Models with Symbolic Solvers for Faithful Logical Reasoning
claim: LLM translates NL to formal spec; deterministic solver infers; solver errors drive refinement: +39.2% over standard prompting, +18.4% over CoT. The external-executor topology H-RULE-CD moves INTO the decode loop.
locator: Abstract (both figures verbatim in the abstract; re-fetched at primary page 2026-07-11).
